item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was recently rereading some of my favorite 5000con entries, and goddamnit am I glad I did; I almost forgot how good of an article this is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is consistent with how Hume levels have been used in the past I think, though I have always found that a bit weird

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really feel much more mysterious than your regular SCP, so I would probably have no-voted during the contest. Coming to it late though, I really enjoyed the setup and the rising tension of the incident log. The ending's a bit anti-climactic, and I found aspects of the AI and the god-thing a bit generic but ah well.

+1 for a solid 7/10 sort of SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Archon means that an object can be contained, but should not be contained due to the issues that containment would cause, to my knowledge/headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what does the archon reclassification signify again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[19:23] Further pressure waves are released from SCP-5001-A in rapid succession. Individuals caught in multiple blasts experienced sudden tumorous growths, loss of higher cognitive function, and reformation of limbs, and began to attack each other. A single vocalization sounds out in an indiscernible voice from the primary containment chamber: "REVERT TO MY DOMAIN."

reject humanity

return to yaldy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you exploring series for introducing me to this article, it's fantastic.

+1 from me, really good job on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also "Premier" in many Western sources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in 1959, President Khrushchev

Either First Secretary or General Secretary. USSR did not have any presidents until Gorbachev.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never did end up changing it, haha. Don't think I could, considering the Exploring Series did a fantastic video on it. Might end up writing a tale after that flips perspective on events, but that's a "later" project rather than a "now" project.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I need to ask.
Did you change the ending afterwards? Or it now is the same as the start? I remember reading it a while back, and didn't see any difference from now. At least I didn't notice one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is really good, I think your work is awesome!!

item.title: Pragmatism?
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of that scene in Indiana Jones where he just shoots the sword guy, but, on a much bigger scale.

10/10 would fools again

+0.5

item.title: April Fools!
item.content:encoded:
A lot of people have been complaining about the ending, so I decided to fix it up. I also change the date to better reflect the modern times or something.

Hope you guys find it improved!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would explain how the detail about Kleiner is relevant, but I can't seem to make the connection between a cybernetic implant and HECOR.

It would make sense to kill The Devourer as soon as it actually breached containment, but it still seems strange that they would first attack the outer walls. If they successfully punched a hole through on the first shot, they'd just release the evil dude before HECOR finished recharging for a second go, and who knows what would happen once it was free. Although on the other hand, they might not have time to react if they waited for it to escape, which was imminent given the "REVERT TO MY DOMAIN". So I'm not sure.

Still, unless the Foundation knew what HECOR would do to the chamber/The Devourer, it seems like a hastily improvised last-ditch attempt at neutralization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is really nicely written! Some big, dramatic SCPs relating to world-ending stuff tend to drag on, overstaying their welcomes and pulling in far too many crosslinks, canons and SCP technobabble terms, but this one manages to remain very concise and enjoyable. The formatting and images only add to that!

However, I think the best SCPs and those that usually get the attention afforded by a significant slot (like X000s) need to offer some good, dramatic twist, or give the reader a good dose of existential dread, which the previous X000s manage to varying levels. 5001 here, while very well written, doesn't really manage to be much more than "a big powerful thing which is so big and powerful it will end the world!!"

+ show block
– hide block

I feel like this SCP offers some really interesting, tantalizing ideas that fail to be utilised properly. E.g. the location is 'biomechanical' but its never elaborated on as to what the biological aspects of it are. 5001-A's ancient age, human temperature and abilities based on a higher hume reading, and big evil bad guy line ('RETURN TO MY DOMAIN') all point clearly towards it being the last remnant of some previous reality that our current one is written over, but this idea is never fully realized in favour of shooting it with a big gun - not that I mind -A getting dabbed on by HECOR, just that in the grand scheme of things it's a rather sudden end that shuts down further exploration of the related ideas.

5001 also suffers from what I feel like are rather clumsy attempts to give it a greater sense of importance and historical weight. A big one is a bunch of Foundation paratechnology 'actually being from here!!' - SCPs which purport to be the "real" origin of other SCPs or certain technologies are a pet peeve of mine because they feel like they're trying to undermine other works on the site, and it's a big reason I'm glad for 'no canon.' The final speech from Omega is trying to do the whole "hello I'm an ancient, benevolent god and I do love humans but I'm afraid i'm just too big and good for you right now and you need to go away so I can do my job" spiel which I feel has become a little overused on the site, and doesn't make much sense at all in this particular case: the Foundation have been able to improve on all the technology they acquired from 5001, and ultimately its one of their own creations that manages to kneecap 5001-A enough to allow its re-containment, but Omega still wants to try the "no no I'm actually better at containment than you guys you don't understand you need to go away!" thing despite the absolute failure of all their own failsafes?

This absolutely doesn't deserve a -1, but ultimately I can't get over the impression that this is a really boring gift wrapped up really nicely in gold leaf and +1 it either, so I'm going to no vote. I'd love to see rewrites of some of the site's older "big bad world ending thing" in this style, with far more fleshed out ideas, containment, nicer formatting, etc., but when it comes to new iterations of that I think it needs to be something more special than a really old evil god in a sphere called 'The Devourer.'


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just my read of the situation so take it with a grain of salt:

It seems to me that the explosion itself was probably related to whatever Kleiner (kek) uploaded into OMEGA, so the explosion, if not anomalous in and of itself, was probably targeting a vulnerable area. As for the satellite, I think the FOundation considered containment more or less breached by then and intended to fire *through* the primary containment chamber and kill YALDABAOTHThe Devourer inside. Gotta crack the shell to get at the nut, after all.

I do agree that it is nice they were able to effectively gobsmack it long enough for 5001 to recontain, and that the Foundation's presence isn't entirely ineffective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a really enjoyable read and left me in awe of the magnitude of the thing. I like that HECOR actually did something useful, as usually the Foundation's technology is described as throwing pebbles at tanks.

I'm confused about the ending though.

It seems a bit strange that a mere explosion did that much damage to 5001 since it's containing a world-ending entity, and I'm not sure how the containment chamber flying into the sky related to 5001-A's escape.

Why did HECOR try to blast open the containment chamber containing The Devourer? (Which, by the way, struck me as an anti-climatic name.) It seemed like HECOR would've released 5001-A from its chamber had 5001 not brought it back down and sealed itself up.

The problem is that the idea has been done before. World-ending entity contained by a struggling benevolent entity. The execution is really good though so I can't complain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, I always try to make my skips visually appealing :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format and CSS combined were very aesthetically pleasing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, that was intentional. I was trying to signify that it was A) organic, and B) somehow intrinsically related to humans. Glad you managed to catch that detail!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't notice any references to either of those GoIs (and I still haven't). What I did notice was that 5001-A's temperature is exactly the same as that of a human. So perhaps I got a different experience out of this than most of you did, but I'm upvoting nonetheless.

item.title: Re: i loved this
item.content:encoded:
+ Collapsibles work like this
- Hope this helps :)
 [[collapsible show="+ Show whatever" hide="- Hide whatever"]] Whatever text to show/hide. [[/collapsible]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the technical descriptions of Foundation tech in this piece, those were really well done. That, and the attention to the media, makes this feel so grounded and real, it's awesome. Great world building over all, I felt that I had a really solid grasp on SCP-5001's structure and design without actually "understanding" it, ya know? The HECOR firing was badass as well (as orbital cannons usually are). The mystery theme was greatly executed as well. Any more info would hurt it, any less would be unsatisfying. Love how there wasn't a single redaction too.

As others have said, the ending did feel like it came a bit too soon, while I like it I feel like it just needed a bit more build up to give it more power. Maybe extend the Foundation's response to the incident a bit more, really hammer home the "that was probably our fault, oops" thing and add some extra intrigue. Still, this pales in comparison to the positives of this article.

the pictures were sexy af, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this, and the buildup to the HECOR firing was awesome. And the picture was really cool, too. Interesting premise and very well-done ending. +1

item.title: Re: i loved this
item.content:encoded: I didn't find any mention of Yaldabaoth. Can you explain how you reached your conclusion?

item.title: Re: +300 AND Third Place
item.content:encoded: yossi time has started, it will never stop

item.title: i loved this
item.content:encoded:
really cool stuff in here i love the log i love the description and the historical background now to be fair maybe i'm a little bit biased because SPOILERS (i don't know how to do collapsibles) The sarkic cults and the church of the broken god are my favorite Gois by Far this was actually one of my 3 picks to win (the others being tufto's proposal and the one that eventually end up winning tahony's proposal) which is funny because the three articles are very different but regardless i'm happy this one got the good response that it did in general i'm waiting to see more of your work in the future!

P.D if i have a complaint and i'm the first to admit is a really minor one is that the article SPOILERS makes Yaldabaoth looks like a bad guy, relating to Yaldabaoth there are different camps and opinions but I'm on the side that basically says that saying that Yaldabaoth is evill is presumptuous for the human race, saying that is like saying that an earthquake is evil or that a hurricane is evil or that the bacteria in our skin thinks we are evil because when we shower we kill millions of them, that said is just different interpretations that's all and again this is a wonderful article and totally deserving of the 5001 slot and i congratulate you in your immense dedication and work in bringing this cool answer to the question ¿ well if mekhane imprisoned Yaldabaoth where the f$#% are them? ¿ is some kind of metaphysical prison or is an actual cage? i love it definitely an upvote for me

item.title: i may suggest...
item.content:encoded:
for yaldabaoth i may suggest reading the sarkicism hub and Spoilers i guess but this article also has a lot of reliance on mekhane and for him/her you could check out the church of the broken god hub really cool stuff in there,
little disclaimer, i'm not trying to gatekeeping you or telling how to interpret this article but as you said it yourself "From the angle if this could be a good article for a newbie to read, I think so. It introduces a lot of terms that are specific in this website, explaining the essential idea" and also the church and the sarkic cults are my favorites Gois so i try to "sell" them to people :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, is the text from the Omega at the last addendum from Pangloss?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This entry is so good, I don't know why it didn't win the first place in the contest. Anyways I loved reading it, so +1 for you :)

item.title: +300 AND Third Place
item.content:encoded:
literally what

Thank you all for this overwhelming response!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be the article against which other "world ending monster keter things" are measured. You broke the mold with this one and while I would love to see follow up articles detailing the construction of this thing or maybe what the devourer is… I kinda think they would take away from the excellence you've crafted here. +1 only because I can't give +10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The issue is that it’s 5000-A’s Hume levels that are rising. As 5000-A’s Hume level rises, 5000-A becomes more powerful and able to bend reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna echo comments about the ending, but the rest of the article (particularly the log!) were so exciting and eminently enjoyable that this is an easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a very series-1 vibe, and not in the good way. There's too much telling and not enough showing. I think the last straw was this truly atrocious line:

Notes: The exact radius which Compound B-705 transmits its effects (as well as its strength at different distances) are incapable of being determined.

I'm sure this could be reworked into something I'd upvote, but as it stands, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternate title: If it ain't broke, don't fix it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this imo should be 5000, i really have nothing else to say
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with the others about the ending, although mine is purely on an aesthetic level. The last note isn't a particularly good payoff, as it seems less like a note from the higher power that's implied and more like a Foundation Monologue. And I got such blue balls from the big hyped up 5000-A just being called The Devourer.

However, the buildup was good enough that I was still satisfied with this article. Enough good shit here for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the half-month later reply, but thanks for replying.

(Originally wrote something more, but my question was answered in another comment chain)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is Archon becoming a thing now?
Had to look up "sacrosanct", but it makes for an appropriate title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is grand, I love it.
A technical point though: “Major tremors are reported at Site-59, equivalent to the epicenter of a 5.0 earthquake.” is inappropriate. The Richter scale measures energy released at the focus of an earthquake, which can be many kilometres below the epicentre. A 5.0 Richter scale earthquake whose focus is 50 km deep feels totally different from that whose focus is 300 km deep at the epicentre.
I suggest using “tonnes of TNT” for scientific accuracy if you are not too keen on comparing to earthquakes.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this! Some people said that it feels the same vibe as 2000 or 3000, but to me, while I was reading, it reminded me of SCP-1000! The idea that there were other advanced civilizations before ours is something that I always found it intriguing… maybe this machine of yours is not even from Earth, who knows?

Also, I really enjoyed the length. I understand that some people enjoy to read lengthy submissions, it makes it stronger when done well, but I prefer the short to middle size ones. You put a lot of information in a small space, and connected with other SCPs lore, in a subtle way.

From the angle if this could be a good article for a newbie to read, I think so. It introduces a lot of terms that are specific in this website, explaining the essential idea, so people don't get lost, or need to read dozens of pages prior to enjoy the skip!

About the nature of the anomaly, it is a good mystery for me! I don't get what is this Yaldabaoth that some people talked about, I never heard about it, so for me is just some god/ older one/ 2317-ish that some ancient civilization was very successful in containing… if the computer can't even explain what it is, or the name of what they contain, it's probably some infohazard, which adds to the power of the creature…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this; very well-constructed, nicely combining pieces of site lore in ways that are obvious in retrospect but which you don't see coming until the end. Good job, Yossi; +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About the Article: What I feel some articles tend to do when addressing something big or noteworthy that ties in to the canons is they become overly long or tend to loose themselves in technical terms related to that canon that readers new or unfamiliar to the canon can get confused with.
This article hits the spot where it is just the right length and just the right amount of tying things into existing canon, without requiring to be proficient in that canon. It is not long or circumstantial but right down to the point, yet technical and intriguing.
I like the way it is written, regardless of the content. Short and crisp. "Big numbers" don't have to be extraordinary long, I think it is more of a challenge to capture the essence of a "big number" in few words, than in an endless wall of text.

About the content: It generally purveys the feeling that there is something big going on, but there are a lot of big and world ending things in the SCP universe and that alone wouldn't make it suitable for a 000 slot.
What does it for me though is that message at the very end, tying it into an established canon, yet leaving enough space for interpretation and expansion. Especially after making the reader wonder what the conclusion will be at first and the suspicious lack of tags for that canon.
While the "Mystery" part got solved a little too easy with that message, I see no failure in sticking to the theme. Didn't the text on the contest page even say: "Now, for SCP-5000, you shall be posing more. Perhaps you'll solve them as well."

As someone who would read this as their first SCP, I would be confused but intrigued none the less.
As someone who has been reading the wiki for years and avidly following the canon about a certain God, this makes me happy to read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of me wanted to rebel at the idea of the Foundation ever taking someone else's word for it that something needs containment without study.

But 6500 SRAs and a whole host of crap they can't even begin to measure let alone understand is pretty convincing. And that's before that pulse-pounding breach log, really some of the most exciting writing you could ask for on this site. I want to see more HECOR. :D +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"metallic device of unknown original"
I think that's a typo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's really wonderful how you built up the mystery and the suspense in an exemplar of the SCP format, complete with graphs, charts, and impersonal timestamped logs. Ultimately, though, I like my Sarkicism and my Broken God to be more mysterious—really beyond human comprehension even when they try to interact with us directly, and quite unable to be contained in physical space, or to speak in clear language about their intentions. It's good! It's just not fully for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun, but went in a direction I’m not a fan of. Not the best execution of contest themes.

I’ll give it a +1 after the contest, but this was more disaster movie than Rama, sadly.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
+ Crit
- Crit

SCP-5000 is a bio-mechanical structure approximately fifty-three kilometers in diameter, located a total of 60 kilometers under northern Russia.

General consensus amongst scientists is to spell out numbers twenty and below and use the Latin symbols for any larger.

cylindrical rods composed of osmium

While Osmium is the densest material in the known universe, it is not suitable for building because it is incredibly brittle. Any containment chamber made out of Osmium would be inadequate for any realistic purpose.

However, all of GRU-P's attempts to penetrate SCP-5000's outer wall resulted in failure, ending in the project being abandoned after two years.

Ending -> resulting

Nonetheless, in 1959, President Khrushchev showed renewed interest in breaching SCP-5000, and funded the operation until he was deposed in 1964.

Nonetheless -> nevertheless
Also, Khrushchev wasn't a president. He was the First Secretary of the Communist Party and Chairman of the Council of Ministers.

The SCP Foundation and GRU-P agreed to keep this technology secret and only accessible among themselves.

Among -> amongst

Scranton Reality Anchors function to alter the total amount of Humes in a given radius of effect

I feel like you could have stopped these descriptions at one line. Reiterating what an SRA is in such detail feels like wasted wordcount to me.

On December 30th, 2019, SCP-5000-A breached.

Breached containment.

That is why it pains me so dearly to request that you leave this place immediately. Your studying and probing have almost resulted in the end of all I had worked towards to keep you alive. If you comply, I guarantee that The Devourer will never escape, and your species will be free to pursue the enlightenment of technology for all eternity.

This really doesn't work for me. It's abundantly clear before this point that our tampering of things is what caused the breach. In addition, I'm not sure I like the implication that SCP-5000-A is Yaldabaoth, the demiurge. The Devourer's continued existence and freedom is integral to the natural state of the universe. The notion that it both has a physical body and is currently in containment really goes against the whole point of the being. Yaldabaoth is supposed to be entropy, a sign of our laws of physics and a symbol of the end. Making it a generic evil god really does it a disservice.

Given all of the above, this is a firm downvote from me. This would need a total overhaul for me to like it.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to TheMightyMcB or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The incident log fully capture that "ohshitohshitohshitohshit" feeling.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think minmin sums up how I feel about this article.

SCP-2217 is about Mekhane, but it's also about the Foundation's relationship with the Veil. SCP-4882 is about Mekhane, but it's also about the interplay between technology and politics. Both articles use our favorite deconstructed deity as a bridge to some deeper truth, not the final destination itself.

This article is about Mekhane, and only Mekhane. There's no underlying message besides the obvious "Mekhane good Yaldabaoth bad", and it feels intellectually empty because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Loved every bit of this other than the last paragraph. Was a little too on the nose for me. Rather leave what is in there a mystery than the big reveal. But that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice article. Yet rather than 5000 "Mystery", I may prefer it to be just an 001 proposal…? Maybe it's due to that the ending is somehow too normal(?), which has been mentioned by other readers above.

Additionally, it was after reading the spoilers that I realized the relationship with 610. I had to say that's quite smart. Anyway, I enjoy this, but regretfully will upvote it when the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just love the incident log. It's wonderful storytelling.

I guess my problem with this is the ending. It's kind of too expected. Something more surprising/unsettling/subtle/implicit would be nice, given that the theme is "Mystery."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this reads like the michael bay of scp 5k submissions. (or rather, the stage directions of it). there's a lot of bright lights and loud noises and big evil and big good and I probably won't remember a single thing about this in a month. I recognise it's growingly difficult to cultivate a unique flavour for original work on this site — there can only be so many judeo christian bad guys, or so many technofuture faceless forerunner/reaper/etc. aliens. but even so, i expect something deserving of the most prominent slot out of the next 1,000 articles to set a better precedence than this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Although, that isn't the interpretarion I had while making the article, I'm a strong believer in Death of the Author, and your interpretation fits just as well. Glad you liked it! :)
